title: Troubleshooting Methodology & Hardware Symptoms
summary: The CompTIA six-step troubleshooting methodology applied to diagnosing and fixing common PC and laptop hardware failures from symptom to root cause.
content:
The DTOT tests your ability to IDENTIFY and SOLVE IT problems, and the CompTIA six-step troubleshooting methodology is the disciplined framework that turns a vague complaint ("it won't turn on") into a confirmed fix. Memorize the steps and their order; the exam frequently asks "what is the NEXT step."

• Step 1 — Identify the problem. Gather information from the user, identify symptoms, ask what changed (new hardware, software, updates, environment), duplicate the problem if possible, and review logs. Critically: back up before making changes, and consider corporate/embassy change-management policies before touching anything.
• Step 2 — Establish a theory of probable cause (question the obvious). Start with the simplest, most likely explanation first ("is it plugged in / powered / connected?"). If needed, conduct external research (vendor KB, event logs) and consider multiple possible causes.
• Step 3 — Test the theory to determine cause. If the theory is confirmed, move to a plan. If it is NOT confirmed, establish a new theory or escalate to a higher tier.
• Step 4 — Establish a plan of action to resolve the problem and identify potential effects. Plan the fix and think through side effects before acting.
• Step 5 — Implement the solution or escalate as necessary.
• Step 6 — Verify full system functionality and, if applicable, implement preventive measures (e.g., dust filters, surge protection, scheduled scans).
• Step 7 (final, ongoing) — Document findings, actions, and outcomes. In a diplomatic post this documentation feeds the ticketing system and the next technician.

NO POWER / NO POST is the first family of hardware faults. No power at all (no fans, no lights) points to the power source: bad wall outlet/UPS, failed power supply (PSU), tripped surge protector, loose 24-pin/8-pin connectors, or a faulty power button/front-panel header. Test with a PSU tester or a known-good PSU; check the voltage selector switch. If fans spin but there is no display and no boot — that is a POST (Power-On Self-Test) failure, not a power failure. The motherboard signals POST errors via beep codes (e.g., on AMI/Award BIOS, repeating beeps often mean RAM; one long + two/three short often means video) and many boards have a two-digit POST code display or POST/diagnostic LEDs (CPU/DRAM/VGA/BOOT). A POST card in a PCI/PCIe slot reads the same code on boards without an onboard display. Always reseat RAM and the GPU, clear CMOS (jumper or remove the coin cell), and strip to minimum hardware (CPU + 1 stick of RAM + onboard/one GPU) to isolate the culprit.

UNEXPECTED SHUTDOWNS AND OVERHEATING. Symptoms: the PC shuts off abruptly under load, throttles (slows down), or reboots when gaming/compiling. Cause is almost always thermal: dust-clogged heatsinks/fans, failed or stopped fan (listen and watch RPM in BIOS/HWMonitor), dried-out or pump-out thermal paste between CPU/GPU and heatsink, blocked vents, or high ambient temperature. Fix: blow out dust with compressed air (hold fans still so they don't over-spin), confirm all fans spin, reapply quality thermal paste after cleaning the old paste with isopropyl alcohol, ensure correct case airflow (intake front/bottom, exhaust rear/top), and check that the CPU cooler is firmly seated. A failing PSU or a thermal-trip motherboard protection can also cause sudden shutdowns; monitor temps to distinguish thermal from electrical.

RAM ISSUES present as random reboots, application crashes, file corruption, Blue Screens of Death (common stop codes: MEMORY_MANAGEMENT, PAGE_FAULT_IN_NONPAGED_AREA, IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL), failure to POST, or a system that boots only sometimes. Likely causes: a failing/marginal DIMM, improperly seated modules, mismatched/incompatible modules, or RAM running above its rated speed (XMP/EXPO instability). Fix: reseat modules, test one stick at a time in the known-good slot to isolate the bad module or slot, run a memory diagnostic (Windows Memory Diagnostic or MemTest86 — let it run multiple passes; any errors mean replace the stick), and confirm the speed/timings are within spec.

STORAGE FAILURES. A mechanical hard drive that is failing often makes a repetitive CLICKING or grinding noise ("click of death"), reads/writes very slowly, throws read errors, or causes the dreaded "No boot device found / Operating System not found" message. SSDs fail more silently — sudden disappearance, read-only mode, or bad blocks. Diagnose with S.M.A.R.T. (Self-Monitoring, Analysis, and Reporting Technology): tools like CrystalDiskInfo or smartctl report reallocated sectors, pending sectors, and a CAUTION/BAD health status — any reallocated/pending sectors trending up means back up NOW and replace the drive. For "no boot device," verify the drive is detected in BIOS/UEFI (if not, reseat SATA/power or M.2; if still missing, the drive is likely dead), confirm the correct boot order and that the boot mode (UEFI vs Legacy/CSM) matches the OS install. A slow but detected drive may just need a cable swap, a defrag (HDD only — never defrag an SSD), or it may be dying. Treat any failing drive as a data-loss emergency: image it before further troubleshooting.

DISPLAY AND VIDEO ISSUES. No image at all: check the monitor power and that the cable is connected to the GPU output (not the disabled motherboard port on a system with a dedicated card), try a different cable/port (HDMI/DP/VGA), and confirm the GPU is seated and has its PCIe power connectors. If onboard video works but the dedicated GPU shows nothing, suspect the card, its power, or its seating. Other symptoms and causes: a DIM display points to a failed backlight/inverter (laptops) or wrong brightness; ARTIFACTS (random colored blocks, lines, garbled textures) indicate an overheating or failing GPU or VRAM, or bad drivers — test by reverting/updating drivers and checking GPU temps; FLICKER often comes from a bad cable, a refresh-rate mismatch, or a failing backlight; DEAD PIXELS (always black) and STUCK PIXELS (always one color) are panel defects (stuck pixels sometimes recover with pixel-massaging utilities; dead pixels usually need panel replacement). Distorted geometry or a buzzing CRT-style image is rare on modern panels but indicates a failing display.

FIRE-HAZARD AND CAPACITOR SYMPTOMS. A BURNING SMELL or visible smoke means power off and unplug immediately — this is a safety issue before it is a repair. Causes include a shorted component, a failing PSU, or burnt traces. SWOLLEN / DISTENDED CAPACITORS (electrolytic caps with bulging or domed tops, sometimes leaking brown/crusty electrolyte) on the motherboard, GPU, or PSU cause instability, random shutdowns, failure to power on, or no POST. The board may work intermittently as caps degrade. Fix: the component must be recapped (specialist soldering) or, more practically in the field, the board/PSU is replaced. Never run hardware with a burning smell or leaking caps. In an embassy, a smoking device also triggers facility safety procedures, not just an IT ticket.

LAPTOP-SPECIFIC FAULTS. Battery not charging: distinguish a dead battery from a charging-circuit/charger problem — test with a known-good OEM charger (wrong-wattage USB-C chargers may power but not charge), check the DC jack for damage, look at the battery health report (powercfg /batteryreport on Windows reports design vs. full-charge capacity; a battery at a fraction of design capacity is worn out), and verify the charging LED behavior. A battery that reads "plugged in, not charging" can be a worn battery, a firmware/EC issue (update BIOS, or drain and reseat), or a bad charger. NO INTERNAL DISPLAY BUT EXTERNAL MONITOR WORKS: the GPU and the system are fine, so the fault is in the laptop's panel path — failed backlight/inverter, a damaged/loose LVDS/eDP ribbon cable (common after a hinge wears), or the panel itself. Confirm by pressing the display-toggle function key and shining a flashlight at the screen (if you can faintly see the desktop, the backlight failed, not the panel data). Also check the lid-close magnetic sensor, which can falsely tell the laptop the lid is shut.
keyPoints: CompTIA 6-step order: identify problem; establish theory (question the obvious); test theory; establish plan; implement/escalate; verify & add preventive measures; then DOCUMENT
Always back up data and consider change-management BEFORE making changes (step 1)
If theory is NOT confirmed when tested, establish a new theory or escalate
No power = power source/PSU; fans spin but no display = POST failure diagnosed via beep codes, POST codes/LEDs, or a POST card
Unexpected shutdowns/throttling = overheating: dust, failed fan, or dried thermal paste — clean and reapply paste
RAM faults cause random reboots, app crashes, and BSODs (MEMORY_MANAGEMENT, IRQL); isolate by testing one stick and run MemTest86
Use S.M.A.R.T. (CrystalDiskInfo/smartctl) to detect failing drives; clicking HDD = imminent failure, back up immediately
'No boot device' = check BIOS detection, SATA/M.2 seating, boot order, and UEFI-vs-Legacy mode match
Artifacts = failing/overheating GPU or VRAM; dim = backlight/inverter; dead pixel (black) vs stuck pixel (one color)
Swollen/distended capacitors cause instability and no-POST; burning smell = power off and unplug immediately (safety first)
Laptop 'plugged in, not charging' = worn battery, wrong-wattage charger, bad DC jack, or EC firmware; check powercfg /batteryreport
Internal display dead but external works = panel path fault (backlight, eDP/LVDS ribbon, or panel) — system/GPU are fine

flashcards.front: List the CompTIA six-step troubleshooting methodology in order.
flashcards.back: 1) Identify the problem; 2) Establish a theory of probable cause (question the obvious); 3) Test the theory; 4) Establish a plan of action and identify effects; 5) Implement the solution or escalate; 6) Verify full functionality and apply preventive measures; then Document findings, actions, and outcomes.

flashcards.front: In step 1 (Identify the problem), what two things must you always consider before making changes?
flashcards.back: Back up data first, and consider corporate/change-management policies. Also gather info, identify symptoms, and ask the user what changed.

flashcards.front: What do you do if testing your theory does NOT confirm the cause?
flashcards.back: Establish a new theory of probable cause, or escalate to a higher support tier.

flashcards.front: PC has no fans, no lights, completely dead. Where do you look first?
flashcards.back: The power source: wall outlet/UPS, surge protector, PSU (test with PSU tester or known-good unit), voltage selector switch, loose 24-pin/8-pin connectors, and the power-button header.

flashcards.front: Fans spin and lights come on but there is no display and no boot. What is this called and how is it diagnosed?
flashcards.back: A POST (Power-On Self-Test) failure. Diagnose via beep codes, the motherboard's two-digit POST code display or diagnostic LEDs (CPU/DRAM/VGA/BOOT), or a POST card in a PCI/PCIe slot.

flashcards.front: A PC shuts down abruptly under heavy load. What is the most likely cause and fix?
flashcards.back: Overheating — dust-clogged heatsink/fans, a failed/stopped fan, or dried-out thermal paste. Fix: blow out dust, confirm fans spin, clean old paste with isopropyl alcohol and reapply, ensure correct airflow and a seated cooler.

flashcards.front: Which BSOD stop codes commonly point to bad RAM?
flashcards.back: MEMORY_MANAGEMENT, PAGE_FAULT_IN_NONPAGED_AREA, and IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL. Symptoms also include random reboots, app crashes, and intermittent boot failures.

flashcards.front: How do you isolate a bad RAM module, and what tool confirms it?
flashcards.back: Reseat modules, then test one stick at a time in a known-good slot. Confirm with Windows Memory Diagnostic or MemTest86 over multiple passes — any error means replace the stick.

flashcards.front: What is S.M.A.R.T. and what does it tell you about a drive?
flashcards.back: Self-Monitoring, Analysis, and Reporting Technology. Tools (CrystalDiskInfo, smartctl) report reallocated/pending sectors and a health status (GOOD/CAUTION/BAD). Rising reallocated/pending sectors mean back up now and replace the drive.

flashcards.front: A hard drive makes a repetitive clicking noise. What does this indicate and what is the priority?
flashcards.back: Imminent mechanical failure (the 'click of death'). Priority is to back up / image the data immediately before any further troubleshooting — then replace the drive.

flashcards.front: Steps for diagnosing a 'No boot device found' error.
flashcards.back: Verify the drive is detected in BIOS/UEFI (if not, reseat SATA/power or M.2 — if still missing, drive is likely dead). Then check boot order and confirm the boot mode (UEFI vs Legacy/CSM) matches the OS installation.

flashcards.front: Why should you never defragment an SSD?
flashcards.back: Defragmentation provides no benefit on flash storage (no read-head seek time) and adds unnecessary write wear, shortening the SSD's life. Defrag applies only to mechanical HDDs.

flashcards.front: Screen shows random colored blocks, lines, or garbled textures. What does this point to?
flashcards.back: Artifacts — typically an overheating or failing GPU/VRAM, or a bad/corrupt video driver. Check GPU temperatures and roll back/update drivers to isolate.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a dead pixel and a stuck pixel?
flashcards.back: A dead pixel is always black (no power to the sub-pixels) and usually needs a panel replacement. A stuck pixel is stuck on one color (red/green/blue) and can sometimes be recovered with a pixel-cycling/massage utility.

flashcards.front: You smell burning or see smoke from a computer. What is the first action?
flashcards.back: Power it off and unplug it immediately — this is a safety issue before it is a repair. In an embassy this also triggers facility safety procedures, not just an IT ticket.

flashcards.front: What do swollen/distended capacitors look like and what symptoms do they cause?
flashcards.back: Electrolytic caps with bulging/domed tops, sometimes leaking crusty brown electrolyte. They cause instability, random shutdowns, failure to power on, or no-POST. Fix: recap (specialist) or replace the board/PSU.

flashcards.front: A laptop says 'plugged in, not charging.' What are the likely causes?
flashcards.back: A worn-out battery, a wrong-wattage/incompatible charger (esp. USB-C), a damaged DC jack, or an embedded-controller/BIOS firmware issue. Check powercfg /batteryreport (design vs. full-charge capacity) and test with a known-good OEM charger.

flashcards.front: The laptop's internal screen is black but an external monitor works fine. What does this tell you?
flashcards.back: The GPU and system are fine; the fault is in the laptop's panel path — failed backlight/inverter, a loose/damaged eDP/LVDS ribbon, or the panel itself. Shine a flashlight at the screen: a faint visible image means the backlight failed (not the data path).

flashcards.front: How do you safely use compressed air on a dusty PC fan?
flashcards.back: Hold the fan blades still so the airflow doesn't over-spin them (which can damage bearings or generate a back-voltage), and blow dust out of heatsinks and vents in short bursts, ideally outdoors or away from the work area.

flashcards.front: What is the recommended way to isolate a no-POST hardware fault on a desktop?
flashcards.back: Strip to minimum hardware: CPU, one stick of RAM in the primary slot, and one GPU (or onboard video). Clear CMOS, reseat components, then add parts back one at a time to find the culprit.

flashcards.front: Why might a system POST and run but with a dedicated GPU showing no image while onboard video works?
flashcards.back: The dedicated card, its seating, or its PCIe power is faulty — or the monitor is plugged into the wrong (disabled motherboard) port. Reseat the card, attach PCIe power, and connect the monitor to the card's output.

title: Network Troubleshooting
summary: The command-line and physical tools for diagnosing network faults, when to use each, and a layered approach mapping connectivity symptoms to causes and fixes.
content:
Network problems are the most common and most visible IT faults at a diplomatic post — when the connection drops, cables stop, VoIP calls and the visa system go down. The exam expects you to know the diagnostic TOOLS, WHEN to use each, and a LOGICAL LAYERED approach that works outward from the local machine.

CORE COMMAND-LINE TOOLS AND WHEN TO USE THEM:
• ping — tests reachability and round-trip latency to a host using ICMP. Use it to confirm a host is up and measure latency/packet loss. Pinging 127.0.0.1 (loopback) tests that the local TCP/IP stack works; pinging the default gateway tests the local link; pinging a public IP (e.g., 8.8.8.8) tests internet routing; pinging a domain name tests DNS plus routing.
• tracert (Windows) / traceroute (Linux/macOS) — shows each hop (router) along the path to a destination and the latency at each. Use it to locate WHERE a path breaks or slows (e.g., timeouts starting at hop 4 = the problem is at/after that router).
• ipconfig (Windows) / ifconfig or ip (Linux/macOS) — shows the interface's IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS. Use it first to verify the machine actually has a valid IP. ipconfig /all shows DHCP and DNS details; ipconfig /release + /renew forces a new DHCP lease; ipconfig /flushdns clears the DNS resolver cache.
• nslookup / dig — query DNS directly to resolve names to IPs and test specific DNS servers. Use them to prove whether DNS is the problem (e.g., nslookup resolves against 8.8.8.8 but not the configured server = the local DNS server is the issue).
• netstat — shows active connections, listening ports, and routing/stats. Use it to see what ports are open/listening and which connections exist (netstat -ano on Windows maps connections to PIDs).

PHYSICAL / LAYER-1 TOOLS:
• Cable tester — verifies a copper cable's wiring (continuity, correct pinout, shorts, opens, split pairs). Use when you suspect a bad or miswired patch/wall cable.
• Loopback plug — feeds a port's transmit back to its own receive to test the NIC/port itself. Use to confirm whether a NIC or switch port is functional.
• Toner probe (tone generator + inductive probe) — injects a tone on one end of a cable so you can find/trace the other end in a bundle or wall. Use to identify which jack/port a cable runs to.
• Wi-Fi analyzer — shows SSIDs, signal strength (RSSI/dBm), and channel usage. Use to find dead spots, co-channel interference, and the least-congested channel.
• Speed test — measures actual download/upload throughput and latency to confirm or quantify a 'slow internet' complaint against the expected bandwidth.

THE LOGICAL LAYERED APPROACH (work outward, isolate the failure point): (1) Loopback — ping 127.0.0.1 to confirm the local TCP/IP stack is alive. (2) Local IP — ipconfig to confirm a valid IP/mask/gateway (not 169.254.x.x). (3) Gateway — ping the default gateway to confirm the local link/switch path. (4) Remote IP — ping a known public IP (8.8.8.8) to confirm internet routing. (5) Name resolution — ping/nslookup a domain name to confirm DNS. Whatever the FIRST step that fails tells you the layer at fault: stack, local config, LAN, WAN/routing, or DNS.

NO CONNECTIVITY. Symptoms: no internet, 'network cable unplugged,' no link lights. Causes/fixes: check physical layer first — cable seated, link LED on, correct port; test the cable with a tester and the NIC with a loopback plug; verify the NIC is enabled and has a driver; ping loopback then gateway to localize. If loopback works but the gateway is unreachable, the fault is the cable, switch port, or local config.

APIPA (169.254.x.x) ADDRESS = DHCP FAILURE. When a Windows client cannot reach a DHCP server it self-assigns an Automatic Private IP Address in the 169.254.0.0/16 range — a clear sign of a DHCP problem, not a hardware problem. Causes: DHCP server down or out of leases, a broken link between client and DHCP server, a disabled/unauthorized DHCP scope, or a blocking switch/VLAN issue. Fix: ipconfig /release then /renew; verify physical connectivity to the DHCP server; check the DHCP scope/leases on the server; on a single host, a static IP can be a temporary workaround.

LIMITED OR INTERMITTENT CONNECTIVITY. 'Limited connectivity' often means a valid link but no full IP/gateway (frequently APIPA or a wrong gateway/mask). Intermittent drops point to a marginal cable, a loose connector, a flaky switch port, duplicate IP addresses, overheating equipment, or (on Wi-Fi) roaming/interference. Fix: reseat/replace cabling, move to another switch port, check for IP conflicts, and review switch/AP logs.

SLOW SPEEDS. Causes: a DUPLEX MISMATCH (one side half-duplex, one side full — causes late collisions, retransmits, and terrible throughput; fix by setting both sides to auto-negotiate or matching them manually); network congestion/oversubscription; a degraded cable forcing a lower link speed (e.g., 100 Mbps on gigabit gear); weak Wi-Fi signal; or an upstream ISP/WAN limit. Diagnose with a speed test (to quantify), check the negotiated link speed/duplex on the switch and NIC, and use tracert to find a slow hop.

DNS FAILURES — the classic 'I can ping the IP but not the name.' If pinging 8.8.8.8 works but pinging google.com fails, routing is fine and DNS is broken. Causes: wrong/unreachable DNS server, a stale or poisoned DNS cache, or a down DNS server. Fix: ipconfig /flushdns, set a known-good DNS server (e.g., the post's resolver or a public one), and verify with nslookup against a specific server.

WIRELESS ISSUES. Low signal: distance, walls/metal/concrete, or AP placement — relocate the AP, add a repeater/mesh node, or move closer. Interference / channel congestion: overlapping 2.4 GHz networks (use non-overlapping channels 1, 6, 11) or microwave/Bluetooth noise — use a Wi-Fi analyzer to pick a clear channel or move to 5 GHz. SSID problems: hidden SSID mistyped, wrong SSID joined, or band-steering confusion. Authentication failures: wrong passphrase, expired credentials, certificate/RADIUS (802.1X/WPA2-Enterprise) issues, or MAC filtering blocking the device. Fix per cause: re-enter credentials, fix the channel, check the RADIUS/cert chain, and confirm the device is permitted.

HIGH LATENCY AND JITTER (VoIP). Voice and video are real-time and sensitive to latency, JITTER (variation in packet delay), and packet loss. Symptoms: choppy/robotic audio, dropped calls, echo, one-way audio. Causes: congestion, no QoS prioritization for voice traffic, a saturated WAN link, Wi-Fi instability, or a duplex mismatch. Fix: implement/verify QoS (prioritize voice VLAN/DSCP EF), reduce congestion, prefer wired over Wi-Fi for phones, and check jitter/latency with continuous ping and call-quality stats. For an embassy's VoIP and secure-comms, QoS and stable low-jitter links are mission-critical.

PORT AND FIREWALL BLOCKS. Symptom: a specific application or service fails while general browsing works (e.g., email, RDP, or a database client times out). Causes: a host or network firewall blocking the port, an ACL on a router/switch, or the service being down. Diagnose: confirm the server is reachable with ping, then test the specific port (telnet host port, Test-NetConnection -Port on Windows, or nmap) — if ping succeeds but the port is closed/filtered, suspect a firewall/ACL. Fix: open the required port on the correct firewall (with proper change approval), verify the service is listening (netstat), and re-test. In a hardened diplomatic network, port blocks are often intentional security controls — confirm policy before requesting a rule change.
keyPoints: ping tests reachability/latency; loopback 127.0.0.1 tests the local stack; pinging the gateway tests the LAN; pinging 8.8.8.8 tests routing; pinging a name tests DNS
tracert/traceroute locates WHERE a path breaks or slows by showing per-hop latency
ipconfig/ifconfig shows IP/mask/gateway/DNS; /release+/renew refreshes DHCP; /flushdns clears the DNS cache
nslookup/dig query DNS directly to prove whether DNS is the fault and test specific DNS servers
Physical tools: cable tester (wiring), loopback plug (NIC/port), toner probe (trace a cable), Wi-Fi analyzer (signal/channels), speed test (throughput)
Layered approach: loopback -> local IP -> gateway -> remote IP -> name; the first failing step names the layer at fault
A 169.254.x.x (APIPA) address means DHCP failure — server down/out of leases or no path to it; fix with release/renew and check the DHCP scope
Can ping the IP but not the name = DNS is broken (routing is fine); flush DNS and set a known-good DNS server
Slow speeds: duplex mismatch (set both to auto/match), congestion, degraded cable forcing lower link speed, or weak Wi-Fi
Wi-Fi: use non-overlapping 2.4 GHz channels 1/6/11 or move to 5 GHz; auth failures = passphrase/RADIUS/cert/MAC filtering
VoIP needs low latency and low jitter; fix with QoS (priority voice VLAN/DSCP EF), less congestion, and wired connections
App works but one service fails = likely a firewall/ACL port block; ping then test the specific port (Test-NetConnection/telnet/nmap)

flashcards.front: What does pinging 127.0.0.1 (loopback) test, and what does it rule out?
flashcards.back: It tests that the local TCP/IP stack and NIC driver are working. Success rules out a broken local stack, so the fault is further out (cable, config, gateway, DNS).

flashcards.front: When do you use tracert/traceroute instead of ping?
flashcards.back: When you need to find WHERE along the path a connection breaks or slows. Tracert shows every hop (router) and the latency at each, so timeouts/high latency starting at a given hop localize the problem to that router or beyond.

flashcards.front: Which command shows a Windows machine's IP, mask, gateway, and DNS, and how do you force a new DHCP lease?
flashcards.back: ipconfig (use ipconfig /all for DHCP/DNS detail). Force a new lease with ipconfig /release then ipconfig /renew.

flashcards.front: What command clears the local DNS resolver cache on Windows, and when would you use it?
flashcards.back: ipconfig /flushdns. Use it when names resolve to stale/wrong IPs or after a DNS change — a classic DNS-troubleshooting step.

flashcards.front: What is nslookup/dig used for in troubleshooting?
flashcards.back: To query DNS directly — resolve a name to an IP and test a specific DNS server. It proves whether DNS is the problem (e.g., resolves against a public server but not the configured one = the local DNS server is at fault).

flashcards.front: What does a cable tester check versus what a loopback plug checks?
flashcards.back: A cable tester verifies the cable's wiring (continuity, pinout, shorts, opens, split pairs). A loopback plug loops transmit back to receive to test the NIC/switch port itself.

flashcards.front: What is a toner probe used for?
flashcards.back: A tone generator injects a signal on one end of a cable and an inductive probe detects it on the other end, letting you trace/identify which jack or port a cable runs to in a bundle or wall.

flashcards.front: What does a Wi-Fi analyzer show and why is it useful?
flashcards.back: It shows nearby SSIDs, signal strength (RSSI/dBm), and channel utilization. Use it to find dead spots, detect co-channel interference, and choose the least-congested channel.

flashcards.front: State the logical layered network troubleshooting sequence.
flashcards.back: Loopback (127.0.0.1) -> local IP (ipconfig) -> default gateway -> remote public IP (8.8.8.8) -> name resolution (nslookup/ping a domain). The first step that fails identifies the layer at fault.

flashcards.front: A client has a 169.254.x.x address. What does this mean and what causes it?
flashcards.back: It is an APIPA address, self-assigned when the client cannot reach a DHCP server. Causes: DHCP server down/out of leases, no path to the DHCP server, or a disabled scope. It signals a DHCP problem, not bad hardware.

flashcards.front: How do you fix an APIPA (169.254) address?
flashcards.back: Run ipconfig /release then /renew, verify physical connectivity to the DHCP server, and check the DHCP scope/leases on the server. A static IP is a temporary single-host workaround.

flashcards.front: What is a duplex mismatch and how does it present?
flashcards.back: One side of a link is half-duplex and the other full-duplex, causing late collisions, retransmissions, and very poor throughput despite a 'connected' link. Fix by setting both ends to auto-negotiate or matching them manually.

flashcards.front: You can ping 8.8.8.8 but not google.com. What is broken?
flashcards.back: DNS — routing/internet connectivity is fine but name resolution is failing. Fix: flush DNS, set a known-good DNS server, and verify with nslookup against a specific server.

flashcards.front: Which 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi channels are non-overlapping, and why does it matter?
flashcards.back: Channels 1, 6, and 11. Using non-overlapping channels avoids co-channel/adjacent-channel interference that degrades throughput and reliability in congested areas.

flashcards.front: List common Wi-Fi authentication failure causes.
flashcards.back: Wrong passphrase, expired or wrong credentials, a RADIUS/802.1X (WPA2-Enterprise) or certificate problem, or MAC-address filtering blocking the device.

flashcards.front: Why is jitter critical for VoIP, and how do you mitigate it?
flashcards.back: Jitter is the variation in packet delay; high jitter makes voice choppy/robotic and drops calls. Mitigate with QoS (prioritize the voice VLAN / DSCP EF), reducing congestion, and using wired connections for phones.

flashcards.front: An app fails to connect to a service but ping to the server works. What do you suspect and how do you confirm?
flashcards.back: A firewall/ACL blocking the specific port. Confirm by testing that port (telnet host port, Test-NetConnection -Port, or nmap): if ping succeeds but the port is closed/filtered, a firewall or ACL is blocking it.

flashcards.front: What is 'limited connectivity' usually a sign of?
flashcards.back: A valid physical link but no full IP/gateway — often an APIPA address or a wrong gateway/subnet mask, meaning the device cannot route beyond the local segment.

flashcards.front: What causes intermittent network drops?
flashcards.back: A marginal/loose cable or connector, a flaky switch port, duplicate IP addresses, overheating equipment, or (on Wi-Fi) interference and roaming. Fix by reseating/replacing cabling, changing ports, checking for IP conflicts, and reviewing logs.

flashcards.front: How does netstat help in troubleshooting?
flashcards.back: It lists active connections, listening ports, and routing/stats. netstat -ano on Windows maps each connection/listening port to a process ID (PID), helping confirm a service is listening and what is using a port.

flashcards.front: Why might pinging a public IP fail while the gateway pings fine?
flashcards.back: The local LAN is healthy but internet/WAN routing is broken — the router's WAN link/ISP is down, or upstream routing/NAT is failing. Use tracert to find where the path stops.

title: Operating System & Software Troubleshooting
summary: Diagnosing and fixing Windows boot failures, performance problems, application crashes, malware, and security symptoms using the CompTIA software and malware-removal best practices.
content:
Software and OS faults are the bulk of help-desk work. The DTOT expects you to fix Windows boot failures, performance problems, application crashes, and security/malware incidents methodically — and to know the CompTIA malware-removal steps in order.

WINDOWS BOOT PROBLEMS. 'Boot device not found / no bootable device / Operating System not found': the firmware can't find a bootable OS. Causes: drive not detected (reseat/replace, or the drive is dead), wrong boot order, a UEFI-vs-Legacy/CSM mismatch with how Windows was installed, or a corrupt boot sector/BCD. Fix from the Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE) with bootrec: bootrec /fixmbr (repairs the Master Boot Record), bootrec /fixboot (writes a new boot sector), bootrec /scanos and bootrec /rebuildbcd (rebuild the Boot Configuration Data store). BSOD / STOP CODES: a fatal kernel error. Read the stop code (e.g., MEMORY_MANAGEMENT, IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL, SYSTEM_THREAD_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED, INACCESSIBLE_BOOT_DEVICE, CRITICAL_PROCESS_DIED) and the failing driver/module if named. Common causes: bad drivers, faulty RAM, failing storage, or recent updates. Fix: boot Safe Mode, roll back/update the offending driver, run sfc and chkdsk, undo recent changes via System Restore, and check RAM/disk. BLACK SCREEN (no desktop after login or after the logo): often a graphics-driver or explorer.exe issue, a pending update, or a corrupt user profile — boot Safe Mode, update/roll back the GPU driver, restart explorer.exe, or restore. SLOW BOOT: too many startup programs/services, a failing drive, pending updates, or fragmentation (HDD) — trim startup items (Task Manager > Startup), check disk health, and let updates finish.

WINDOWS RECOVERY AND REPAIR TOOLS — know what each does: Safe Mode loads a minimal driver/service set to isolate whether a third-party driver or app is the culprit. The Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE) provides Startup Repair, System Restore, Command Prompt, and uninstall-updates options. sfc /scannow (System File Checker) scans and repairs corrupt protected Windows system files from a cached copy. DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth repairs the underlying component store that sfc relies on (run DISM first if sfc can't fix files). chkdsk /f /r checks the file system for errors and recovers readable data from bad sectors. System Restore rolls the OS (system files, registry, drivers) back to an earlier restore point WITHOUT touching personal files. Startup Repair auto-diagnoses common boot issues. WINDOWS UPDATE FAILURES (stuck downloads, error codes, reboot loops): causes include a corrupt update cache, low disk space, or a conflicting component. Fix: run the Windows Update Troubleshooter, stop services and clear the SoftwareDistribution folder, free disk space, run sfc/DISM, and retry; if an update bricks the system, uninstall it from WinRE.

PERFORMANCE ISSUES. Diagnose with Task Manager and Resource/Performance Monitor to find which resource is the bottleneck. HIGH CPU: a runaway process, malware, a stuck Windows Update/search-indexer, or insufficient hardware — identify the process and stop/uninstall/limit it. HIGH RAM (low memory, paging, sluggishness): too many apps, a memory leak, or too little RAM — close/restart the offending app or add RAM. HIGH DISK (100% disk usage, especially on HDDs): heavy indexing, Superfetch/SysMain, telemetry, a failing drive, or swapping due to low RAM — check disk health (S.M.A.R.T.), manage indexing/services, and consider an SSD upgrade. STARTUP BLOAT: disable unneeded startup apps in Task Manager > Startup and unnecessary auto-start services (services.msc / msconfig). The general approach: identify the bottleneck resource first, then the specific process, then remediate (close, disable, update, or upgrade).

APPLICATION CRASHES, HANGS, AND COMPATIBILITY. An app that crashes (closes unexpectedly), hangs (not responding), or won't launch: check the Windows Event Viewer (Application log) and Reliability Monitor for the faulting module. Causes/fixes: corrupt app files (repair/reinstall), missing/old runtime (install/update .NET, Visual C++ redistributables), corrupt user profile/settings (test a new profile or clear the app cache), permissions (run as admin where appropriate), or a conflicting add-in/driver. COMPATIBILITY: a legacy app failing on a newer Windows — use Compatibility Mode (run as an older Windows version), run as administrator, or use a VM/virtualization for unsupported software. A hung app can be force-closed via Task Manager (End Task).

MALWARE REMOVAL — the CompTIA best-practice steps IN ORDER (high-value exam item): (1) Identify and verify malware symptoms; (2) Quarantine the infected system (disconnect it from the network to stop spread/exfiltration); (3) Disable System Restore (in Windows) so the malware isn't preserved in restore points; (4) Remediate the infected system — update the anti-malware definitions, then scan and remove using appropriate techniques (Safe Mode, pre-installation/bootable environment as needed); (5) Schedule scans and run updates, and re-enable System Restore (and create a fresh restore point); (6) Educate the end user to prevent recurrence. Memorize the order — disabling System Restore comes BEFORE remediation, and re-enabling it comes AFTER, and quarantine (disconnect) comes early.

SECURITY SYMPTOMS AND THEIR LIKELY CAUSES. POP-UPS and BROWSER REDIRECTS: adware, a malicious browser extension, or a hijacked homepage/search — remove the extension, reset the browser, and run a malware scan. ROGUE / FAKE ANTIVIRUS ('your PC is infected, call this number / pay now'): scareware malware — do not call/pay; remove it with a legitimate scanner (often from Safe Mode). CERTIFICATE WARNINGS ('your connection is not private', NET::ERR_CERT errors): an expired/untrusted/mismatched certificate, a wrong system clock, or a man-in-the-middle/interception — check the date/time first, verify the site's cert, and never bypass warnings on sensitive sites. ACCOUNT LOCKOUTS: repeated bad passwords (often a brute-force attempt or a service using stale cached credentials) — check security logs, identify the source, and reset/unlock; recurring lockouts can indicate an attack. UNEXPECTED DATA-USAGE SPIKES (especially mobile/endpoints): malware exfiltrating data, a compromised app, or background updates — investigate which app/process is transmitting and scan for malware. RENAMED/ENCRYPTED FILES with ransom notes = ransomware: isolate immediately, do not pay, and restore from known-good backups. Other red flags: disabled security tools, new unknown user accounts, unexpected services, and altered host files.

MOBILE AND APP TROUBLESHOOTING. Common mobile faults and fixes: an app crashing or freezing (force-close and reopen, clear the app cache/data, update or reinstall the app); poor battery life or overheating (identify a battery-draining/rogue app, close background apps, check for a stuck radio); no/poor connectivity (toggle airplane mode, re-pair Wi-Fi/Bluetooth, reset network settings); unresponsive device (soft reset/reboot, then a factory reset as a last resort after backup); slow performance or low storage (clear cache, remove unused apps); and OS/app updates that fix bugs and security holes. For SECURITY on mobile, watch for the same signals — unexpected pop-ups, high data usage, rapid battery drain, and unknown apps can indicate a malicious app; remove suspicious apps, keep the OS/apps updated, and use only trusted app stores. In a diplomatic context, also enforce MDM policies, remote-wipe capability for lost/stolen devices, and approved-app restrictions.
keyPoints: Boot device not found = drive not detected, wrong boot order, UEFI/Legacy mismatch, or corrupt BCD; repair with bootrec /fixmbr, /fixboot, /scanos, /rebuildbcd in WinRE
Read BSOD stop codes (MEMORY_MANAGEMENT, IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL, INACCESSIBLE_BOOT_DEVICE) to point at drivers, RAM, or storage
Safe Mode loads minimal drivers to isolate a faulty driver/app; WinRE offers Startup Repair, System Restore, and Command Prompt
sfc /scannow repairs system files; run DISM /RestoreHealth first if sfc fails; chkdsk /f /r fixes the file system and bad sectors
System Restore rolls back system files/registry/drivers WITHOUT deleting personal files
Performance: find the bottleneck resource in Task Manager/Resource Monitor first, then the process; high disk often = indexing/SysMain or a failing drive
Reduce slow boot/startup bloat by disabling startup apps (Task Manager > Startup) and unneeded auto-start services
App crashes/hangs: check Event Viewer + Reliability Monitor; fix via reinstall, runtime updates, new profile, permissions, or Compatibility Mode for legacy apps
Malware removal order: 1 identify/verify, 2 quarantine (disconnect), 3 disable System Restore, 4 remediate (update + scan/remove), 5 schedule scans & re-enable System Restore, 6 educate the user
Security symptoms: pop-ups/redirects = adware/extensions; fake AV = scareware (don't pay); cert warnings = expired/untrusted cert or wrong clock; lockouts = brute force/stale credentials
Ransomware (encrypted files + ransom note): isolate immediately, do not pay, restore from known-good backups
Mobile: clear cache/reinstall for app crashes; watch data spikes/battery drain/unknown apps as malware signs; enforce MDM and remote wipe in diplomatic settings

flashcards.front: What are the bootrec commands and what does each do?
flashcards.back: bootrec /fixmbr repairs the Master Boot Record; bootrec /fixboot writes a new boot sector; bootrec /scanos finds Windows installations; bootrec /rebuildbcd rebuilds the Boot Configuration Data store. Run them from the Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE).

flashcards.front: What causes a 'Boot device not found / Operating System not found' error?
flashcards.back: The drive isn't detected (reseat/replace), wrong boot order, a UEFI-vs-Legacy/CSM mismatch with the install, or a corrupt boot sector/BCD. Verify detection in firmware, fix boot order/mode, then repair with bootrec.

flashcards.front: What is Safe Mode and why is it useful in troubleshooting?
flashcards.back: It boots Windows with a minimal set of drivers and services, isolating whether a third-party driver, startup app, or service is causing crashes/black screens — so you can roll back drivers, remove apps, or scan for malware in a clean state.

flashcards.front: Difference between sfc /scannow and DISM /RestoreHealth?
flashcards.back: sfc /scannow scans and repairs corrupt protected system files from a local cached copy. DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth repairs the underlying component store that sfc draws from — run DISM first if sfc reports it cannot fix files.

flashcards.front: What does chkdsk /f /r do?
flashcards.back: /f fixes file-system errors and /r locates bad sectors and recovers readable information. It is the go-to for file-system corruption and disk errors (often needed before bootrec on a corrupt volume).

flashcards.front: Does System Restore delete your personal files?
flashcards.back: No. System Restore rolls back system files, the registry, installed drivers, and programs to an earlier restore point, but it does not touch personal documents/data.

flashcards.front: A Windows Update is stuck or failing with an error code. How do you fix it?
flashcards.back: Run the Windows Update Troubleshooter, stop the update services and clear the SoftwareDistribution (download cache) folder, free disk space, run sfc/DISM, then retry. If an installed update broke the system, uninstall it from WinRE.

flashcards.front: How do you approach a general 'computer is slow' performance complaint?
flashcards.back: Open Task Manager / Resource Monitor and find the bottleneck resource (CPU, RAM, or disk) first, then identify the specific process driving it, then remediate — close/uninstall/limit the process, disable startup bloat, add RAM, or upgrade to an SSD.

flashcards.front: What commonly causes 100% disk usage on Windows, especially on HDDs?
flashcards.back: Heavy search indexing, SysMain/Superfetch, telemetry, swapping due to low RAM, or a failing drive. Check S.M.A.R.T. health, manage indexing/services, and consider an SSD upgrade.

flashcards.front: Where do you look first to diagnose an app that keeps crashing, and what are common fixes?
flashcards.back: Event Viewer (Application log) and Reliability Monitor for the faulting module. Fixes: repair/reinstall the app, install/update runtimes (.NET, Visual C++), test a new user profile or clear cache, adjust permissions, or remove a conflicting add-in.

flashcards.front: How do you run a legacy application that won't work on a newer Windows?
flashcards.back: Use Compatibility Mode (run as an earlier Windows version), run as administrator if needed, or run it in a virtual machine/virtualized environment for unsupported software.

flashcards.front: List the CompTIA malware-removal best-practice steps IN ORDER.
flashcards.back: 1) Identify and verify malware symptoms; 2) Quarantine the infected system (disconnect from network); 3) Disable System Restore (Windows); 4) Remediate — update anti-malware definitions, then scan and remove; 5) Schedule scans/updates and re-enable System Restore; 6) Educate the end user.

flashcards.front: In malware removal, why disable System Restore before remediating and re-enable it after?
flashcards.back: Disabling it first prevents the malware from being preserved in restore points (and prevents reinfection from a restore). Re-enabling it afterward — with a fresh clean restore point — restores normal recovery protection on a now-clean system.

flashcards.front: Why is quarantine (disconnecting from the network) an early malware step?
flashcards.back: It stops the malware from spreading to other systems and from exfiltrating data or receiving commands while you work on it — containment comes before remediation.

flashcards.front: A user sees constant pop-ups and their browser redirects to strange sites. Cause and fix?
flashcards.back: Adware, a malicious browser extension, or a hijacked homepage/search engine. Remove the offending extension, reset the browser settings, and run a full malware scan.

flashcards.front: What is rogue/fake antivirus (scareware) and how do you handle it?
flashcards.back: Malware posing as a security alert ('your PC is infected — pay/call now') to extort or to install more malware. Do not call or pay; remove it with a legitimate scanner, often from Safe Mode.

flashcards.front: A browser shows 'your connection is not private' / certificate warning. What should you check?
flashcards.back: Check the system date/time first (a wrong clock invalidates certs), then verify the certificate is valid/trusted and matches the site. The cause is an expired/untrusted/mismatched cert or possible interception — never bypass it on sensitive sites.

flashcards.front: What can repeated account lockouts indicate, and how do you investigate?
flashcards.back: Repeated bad passwords from a brute-force attempt or a service/app using stale cached credentials. Check security/audit logs to find the source, reset/unlock the account, and treat recurring lockouts as a possible attack.

flashcards.front: Encrypted files with a ransom note appear. What do you do?
flashcards.back: Ransomware — isolate the system from the network immediately, do not pay the ransom, and restore from known-good (offline/tested) backups. Report per incident-response policy.

flashcards.front: An app on a phone keeps crashing. What is the standard fix sequence?
flashcards.back: Force-close and reopen the app, clear its cache (then data if needed), check for and install an app update, and as a last resort uninstall and reinstall it. A device reboot can also clear transient issues.

flashcards.front: Which mobile symptoms can indicate a malicious app?
flashcards.back: Unexpected pop-ups, unexplained high data usage, rapid battery drain/overheating, unknown installed apps, and degraded performance. Remove suspicious apps, update the OS/apps, and install only from trusted app stores.

flashcards.front: What mobile-device security controls matter in a diplomatic/enterprise setting?
flashcards.back: Mobile Device Management (MDM) policy enforcement, remote-wipe capability for lost/stolen devices, approved-app/app-store restrictions, encryption, and keeping the OS and apps patched.
